schools.summary: The visit to Nazareth, distinct from the earlier rejection (Luke 4:16-31); a second chance for his home town (Matthew 13:54-58).
Theophylact: he returned that they might have no excuse; Bede on the Nazarenes' blindness despising him for his kindred; Pseudo-Jerome: a man's origin breeds contempt (1 Samuel 25:10).
Henry: though endangered there (Luke 4:29), Christ came again — so he waits to be gracious; the disciples followed into danger.
The curt 'Whence these things?' with a sting; 'the many' amazed yet challenging his wisdom and works (reported, since none done at Nazareth); taught on the sabbath.

outliers.note: The note here concerns the sealing of the sepulchre, not this verse.

schools.summary: Mark alone calls Jesus 'the carpenter' (Matthew 13:55 'the carpenter's son'); tekton a worker in wood; the Jewish maxim that a father must teach a trade.
Gill: great rabbis plied trades, so no ground to despise him; he worked to bear part of Adam's curse.
Wesley: no doubt he wrought with Joseph in his youth.
The proverb of the prophet without honour at home (Matthew 13:57; John 4:44) explains the Nazarenes' rejection; Mark adds 'among his own kin, and in his own house'.
Gill: Christ answered their offence with the proverb.
Mark alone adds the laying on of hands on a few sick; the laying-on detail peculiar to Mark.
Gill: the Hebrew idiom 'could not' means 'not fit and proper', not that he lacked power (Genesis 19:22; Mark 9:23).
Wesley: he could not work miracles there consistently with his wisdom and goodness, which would only increase their condemnation.
His marvelling at unbelief bears on the reality of his human nature; he marvelled at the centurion's faith (Luke 7:9) and here at its lack at home.
Theophylact: sow the word in lowly villages, sending two-by-two (Ecclesiastes 4:9); Bede: he gave his servants his own healing virtue.
Paired two-by-two for mutual support and union; six pairs covering Galilee; Mark omits the discourse of Matthew 10, being the Evangelist of action; the imperfect 'kept on giving' authority.
Gregory: sent two-by-two because charity needs two — none without love of neighbour should preach.
Henry: they received that they might give, learned that they might teach, now set to work.
The staff discrepancy (Mark allows, Matthew 10:10 / Luke 9:3 forbid) resolved by 'no second staff' or differing emphases; 'no money' = no brass coin (Mark 12:42); Mark omits the ban on Gentiles.

debates.question: Did Jesus permit a staff (Mark) or forbid it (Matthew, Luke)?

sides.position: No second staff — a single staff allowed
Differing emphases of one command to austere simplicity

outliers.note: The note here concerns a salutation, not this verse.

schools.summary: The sandal (Acts 12:8), lighter wear of the poor, distinguished from the 'shoes' forbidden in Matthew 10:10; two coats a sign of wealth.
Wesley: the mission initiated them into apostolic work, teaching them to trust God's care (Luke 22:35).
Robertson: abide in one house, avoiding a restless manner and choosing a home with care (Matthew 10:11; Luke 9:4); not a ban on accepting invitations.
Gill: Christ continued his directions to them.
'Shake off the dust' as a testimony against the rejecters (Luke 9:5; Acts 13:51); 'whatsoever place' the better text; the Sodom clause transferred from Matthew 10:15.
The Cyril of Jerusalem entry is a catechetical passage on eternal life, tangential to the verse.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on eternal life, not on the verse.

schools.summary: They preached repentance, the message of the Baptist (Matthew 3:2) and Jesus (Mark 1:15); a continuation of John's work, not yet proclaiming Jesus as the Christ (Ellicott).
Calvin: the apostles executed a temporary commission, going through the villages and soon returning.
Mark alone mentions anointing with oil (cf. James 5:14); debated as medicine or symbol of divine healing (John 9:6); with no affinity to extreme unction.
Clarke and Wesley: the oil availed no more of itself than the laying-on of hands; the cure was by God's supernatural blessing.

debates.question: Was the anointing with oil medicine or symbol?

sides.position: A symbol of miraculous power, not a medicament
Uniting natural means with prayer for blessing

schools.summary: The tour spread Christ's fame to the palace; Herod's guilty 'theology' that miracles 'work in' a risen John (John 10:41); Persius on Herod's birthday dread; full account in Matthew 14.
Bede on Jewish envy believing John risen but not Jesus; Theophylact/Pseudo-Chrysostom: the people veil under Moses' prophet (Deuteronomy 18:15) their fear to confess the Christ.
Henry surveys the wild notions of the people, who would believe anything but that he was the Son of God.

outliers.note: The note here concerns Pilate and the governor, not this verse.

schools.summary: The rival opinions — Elijah, or a prophet like the ancients (Matthew 16:14); Clarke's textual note on omitting 'or'.
Wesley: 'a prophet, as one of the prophets' = not inferior to the ancient prophets.
Robertson: Herod's fears settled on this nightmare, the charger in his dreams; 'whom I beheaded' shows he recognises his guilt; Wesley: he still said 'It is John'.
Mark recounts John's death before Herod's fears; the murder at Machaerus ended the tour (Matthew 14:12); fuller notes at Matthew 14:3-12.
Bede on the unlawful marriage; Theophylact on the fury of lust; Gregory's wonder that the greatest prophet dies for a girl's dancing; Pseudo-Jerome allegorises the head of the Law.
Gill and the Gloss: Herod feared John, knowing him just and holy.
The marriage forbidden by Leviticus 18:16; unlawful while the brother lived (Leviticus 20:21); John told Herod plainly to his face (Gill).
The Cyril of Jerusalem entry on the apostles' dignity is tangential to the verse.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the apostles' dignity, not on the verse.

schools.summary: Herodias 'had it in for him' (Robertson) — the imperfect of settled, persistent hostility (Genesis 49:23); 'could not' marks only delay; she sought to kill him.
Gill: she bore an inward grudge and lay in wait for the first opportunity.
The portrait peculiar to Mark — 'just' toward men, 'holy' toward God, 'much perplexed' (better text); the prison interviews bracing Herod's jaded mind (Robertson).
Barnes and Wesley: Herod did many things but not the one required — a 'delusive joy' hearing John gladly while Herodias lay in his bosom.
Gill: reverence and fear of the multitude alike restrained him.

outliers.note: The note here concerns baptizing the nations, not this verse.

schools.summary: The 'well-appointed day' Herodias awaited; the guests the magnates, chiliarchs, and chief men of Galilee; the birthday feast (Genesis 40:20).
Gill: the festal mirth and the king's cups gave Herodias her opportune time.
The textual variants on the dancer's name; Herodias degraded her own daughter to a lascivious dance before drunken men (Isaiah 3:16); Gill names her Salome, Philip's daughter.
The swaggering oath 'unto the half of my kingdom' echoes Ahasuerus to Esther (Esther 5:3; 7:2); Clarke on eastern extravagance toward dancers.
Gill: Herod added an oath to confirm and encourage her; Wesley: a proverbial expression of liberality.
Her going to ask proves she was not told beforehand (Robertson); the consultation peculiar to Mark; she withdrew, women not sitting at the feast.
Calvin: Herodias acted not from mere revenge but the dread of being cast off, hoping the prophet's blood would secure her position.
'Straightway with haste' before the king's mood passed (Robertson); 'by and by' meant 'forthwith' in old English; she returned with eager diligence, hastened by her mother.
Herod caught between conscience and environment, which stifled it (Robertson); the guests glad to see the prophet extinguished (Clarke).
Calvin: he repents of his levity but lacks courage to refuse, as if retracting a rash promise were worse than a heinous crime; Wesley: his honour like the chief priests' conscience (Matthew 27:6).

outliers.note: The note here is misplaced, not bearing on the verse.

themes: an oath no warrant for sin

schools.summary: Mark's Latin word speculator — a bodyguard soldier used as courier and executioner (Suetonius, Tacitus); Lightfoot on the Targum transliteration; one of his guard.
Gill: the king sent the executioner immediately, the prison being near.

Gill: the head brought in a dish and passed to the girl and her mother; Calvin: the holy man's head made a matter of sport, yet his blood precious to God (Psalm 116:15), and Herodias later ruined in banishment.
John's disciples bury their executed master and go to tell Jesus (Matthew 14:12) — a mournful time, turning to the One John announced.
Calvin: the burial honours the dead as a token of the resurrection, attests the disciples' continued loyalty to John's doctrine, and braves the tyrant's rage — a praiseworthy, courageous confession.
The Twelve return from their first mission and report all to Jesus, who listened attentively (Luke 9:10); near the same time John's death is told.
The sent ones return to the fountain-head to give thanks and report (Pseudo-Jerome); we must not overstep our office but report often — not only teach but act (Theophylact).
Henry: ministers are accountable for both deeds and doctrine — let them do and teach only what they are willing to lay open before Christ.
Christ calls the over-wrought apostles to rest — a needed lesson for all preachers of occasional refreshment, which even Jesus felt the need of (Robertson, Clarke).
Henry: a tender call to come apart, alone, to a desert place, for a little while — the full rest reserved for heaven; those set over others deserve rest (Theophylact).
Christ and the Twelve withdraw by ship to a desert place near Bethsaida for rest and privacy — the voyage sparing the toil of the road (Henry); the privacy soon broken by the crowd.

The crowds recognize Jesus and outrun the boat on foot from all the cities, waiting when he lands (Robertson) — their breathless pursuit excused by fulness of affection (Henry); a trial of faith met (Bede).
Christ, instead of resenting the intrusion on his rest, is moved with compassion at a leaderless people (the rabbis blind leaders) and teaches them many things (Robertson).
Theophylact: the Pharisees were ravening wolves devouring the sheep; they gather to Christ the true Shepherd, who feeds them with the word of God.
Henry: he was moved with compassion, not displeasure — the sheep willing to be taught but shepherdless; he healed and taught them.
The day far spent (after 3 P.M.), the disciples pity the crowd; Christ calls them to feed the people that they may testify they have no bread, magnifying the miracle (Bede).

The five loaves = the books of Moses, two fishes = Psalms and Prophets, opened by Christ and distributed through the apostles; twelve baskets = the Church's teachers (Bede); the ranks = the churches of the one Catholic (Gregory).
Theophylact: the disciples grow in love, interceding for the crowd; Christ tries their faith and, looking to heaven, teaches us to seek food from God, giving with superabundant profusion.
The disciples propose sending the crowd to the farms and villages to buy bread — a kind but limited compassion (Henry), not grasping that Christ could feed them where they sat.

'Give ye them to eat' tests their faith; '200 pennyworth' (a famous rabbinic sum, likely their whole purse) — Philip's incredulous reckoning (John 6:7) exposes weak faith, echoing Moses (Numbers 11:22).
'How many loaves?' tries their faith further and makes the miracle undeniable (Gill); the five barley loaves and two fishes — a lad's lunch (John 6:9) — a slender store magnifying the miracle.
Christ seats the crowd in orderly companies on the green grass — a vivid Markan/Petrine touch marking the spring, near Passover (John 6:4); the God of order, that none be overlooked (Henry).

The crowd sat in ranks 'like garden beds' (Peter's vivid memory), by hundreds and fifties (~5,000) — colour and order combined, recalling rabbinic school seating; the green grass marks Passover (Robertson).
Christ takes, looks up, blesses, breaks, and gives through the disciples; the blessing the means of the multiplying (Henry); thanksgiving due to God who gives (Clarke).

Looking to heaven teaches us to seek food from God and shows Christ at one with the Father; distribution through the disciples makes them witnesses and figures their office of dispensing to the world (Chrysostom, Bede).
'All eat, and were filled' — the proof and measure of the miracle; from five loaves five thousand fully satisfied, Christ the great Feeder in whom is enough for all (Henry; cf. Deuteronomy 8:3).
Twelve baskets of fragments (more than the original store) prove the superabundant power of Christ; a lesson against wasting God's gifts, mindful of the needy (Henry).

Each apostle bears a basket as witness of the unspeakable miracle; Christ's gifts are lavish unlike Moses' manna or Elijah's measured store; the twelve baskets figure the apostles and teachers (Chrysostom, Bede).

Five thousand 'men' (excluding women and children) fed from five loaves — a nature miracle only God can work, recorded by all four Gospels and three eyewitnesses, overwhelming proof of Christ's divinity (Robertson, Clarke).
Christ urgently sends the disciples ahead and dismisses the crowd to defuse the move to make him king (John 6:15); the two-Bethsaida question explains the geography (Ellicott).

Walking on the waves proves Christ Master of the elements and true God in real flesh (Bede, against Theodore of Pharan); the tossed ship figures the Church striving through the world's storms toward her rest.
Henry: Christ prays alone on the mountain while the Church is tossed — a comfort that the Master intercedes; his delayed coming meets their extremity; 'It is I' makes them cheerful.
Christ withdraws to the mountain to pray alone (cf. Mark 1:35) — following the dismissal of the king-making crowd (John 6:15); a model of secret devotion before the night's work.
Henry: though sinless he prayed alone to set us an example and encourage dependence on his intercession — 'a good man is never less alone than when alone with God.'
At nightfall the ship is mid-sea, Christ alone on the land (a vivid Markan/Petrine touch) — he watches his disciples toil from the shore, present in his gaze before he comes (John 6:17).
Bede: the lone ship and Christ on land figure the Church seemingly deserted in tribulation, yet seen and strengthened by her Redeemer's look of love.

outliers.note: The Cyril fragment here, on the firmament of creation, is an off-topic catechetical aside, not on this verse.

themes: ship in the midst of the sea

schools.summary: Christ sees them distressed against the contrary wind, comes in the fourth watch (3-6 A.M.) walking on the sea; 'would have passed by them' (only in Mark) — a delay meeting their extremity (Henry).
He seems to pass by to wring from them the cry for help (Augustine); he let them toil all night to teach patience and to wait for help in tribulation (Theophylact).
The disciples take Christ for a spirit and cry out in terror — the natural reaction of frightened men in the dark (Henry; cf. Luke 24:37); they mistook their Saviour for a phantom.
All saw and were troubled; Christ's voice reassured them — 'It is I' both reveals him and dispels terror; they knew his voice as sheep their shepherd (John 10:4).
Henry: 'I am he' is God's delivering name (Exodus 3:14) — it strikes down enemies (John 18:6) but raises up and comforts those who come by faith; to know Christ near is cheer in any storm.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here, on teaching (Matthew 7:28-29), belongs elsewhere, not this verse.
The Cyril fragment here, on the Spirit breathed on the apostles, is an off-topic catechetical aside.

themes: it is I, be not afraid
they all saw and were troubled

schools.summary: Christ enters the ship and the wind ceases at once — by his presence, not a formal rebuke as in Mark 4:39 (Henry); Mark omits Peter's faltering walk (perhaps his own humility, Robertson).
Theophylact: entering the ship he restrains the tempest; the storm made the miracle greater, giving the apostles a fuller knowledge of his power than the loaves had.

themes: he went up into the ship

schools.summary: Their excessive amazement sprang from not learning from the loaves; 'hardened' means slow, dull apprehension (not hostility or reprobation), failing to reason that all nature is subject to Christ (Barnes, Wesley); Peter's candid memory (Ellicott).
Henry: had they weighed the loaves rightly, the walking on the sea would not have so astonished them — we are over-amazed at Christ's works for want of learning from the former ones.
They land at the fertile plain of Gennesaret ('prince's garden') and moor the boat, driven south of Bethsaida by the night wind (Robertson).

The people's great faith (kindled by the report of the woman with the issue of blood, Mark 5) brings the sick to Christ; the hem read as his least commandment or his incarnate flesh (Theophylact, Bede).
The people 'straightway knew him' — by sight and the fame of his miracles, for he had been there before (Barnes, Clarke); recognition moves them to seize the opportunity (Henry).

The people run through the whole region, carrying the sick on pallets (cf. Mark 2:4) to wherever Christ was (Robertson) — an eager, energetic faith that spares no labour to bring the needy to the Healer (Henry, Bede).

Wherever Christ went the sick were laid in the marketplaces to touch even the hem (fringe, Numbers 15:38) of his garment, and all who touched were healed (recurring tense); his tireless going about a pattern for ministry (Clarke).
The touch of the hem, learned from the woman with the issue of blood (Mark 5), read mystically as Christ's incarnate flesh by which we come to the Word (Theophylact).
Henry: they sought only healing for the body, not teaching for the soul — a sad reminder that men care more for the body than the soul.

themes: touch the border of his garment
as many as touched were made whole
